speaker: interviewer
text: Leon? Where did you go when you left the hospital in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left the hospital. They took me back to the same work-- to the same work. And I didn't-- I couldn't do it anymore, and I-- so they beat me up and thing, and staying in the cold. It was still winter. So I got pneumonia. And I had a very high temperature. I couldn't eat. So in the evening, I went down to the hospital again. And so they put in the thermometer, and so my temperature, they took me right away to the hospital. And I was laying there about [INAUDIBLE], without eating. Three days I couldn't eat anything. And they put me-- cold towels around So I didn't have any medication. The same doctor didn't come in. But just was passing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I saw there was a-- a nurse, a man, a nurse, also a prisoner, a Polack. And I told him, listen, go down-- the main doctor-- you know who the main doctor is? Go tell him that I am here. And I'm sick with pneumonia. I have a very high temperature. Maybe he's going to give me some medication. They aren't going to give me anything. So he said, you're kidding. I should go to him? He would not talk to me. I said, listen to me. He knows me. So finally, he decided he's going to go down. He went down and talked to him. He came in right away and he saw me. He said, Leon, I'm going to bring you some medication. He brought me three aspirins. And I took the three aspirins in the evening. And I start to sweat all night.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, the temperature was gone. I start to get appetite to eat. So I start to eat. And I-- and been there another three days, he came in. He said, I can't keep you any longer. But you don't go to work. You go to the recovery barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came in there with about 500 people. And Mengele came every second day and took out 150 people. So I say to the doctor, listen, I feel good. I want to go back to work because I don't want to lay here. I don't want to get killed yet. So it was also a Polish doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, listen to me. You can't go to work after pneumonia. You have to be here 28 days. I say, look what's happening-- he take out 150 people every day. He said he will not touch you. Listen to me. In the meantime, I mean I got good relations with him. I started to sew for him. And I was there 28 days. And then he kept me another 28 days. When Mengele came in, he put me in his office. And when he went away, I went back to bed. And he kept me over the whole winter. In the meantime, I had a friend outside. What he was in Canada now-- he died about two years ago. He was my good friend. He was like a relative. Not a close relative.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came. He said, Leon. I have for you-- when you go out from the hospital, I have for you a kapo, what he going to take you in on his job and you aren't going to work outside anymore. But you have to have from the doctor a paper, or you can't work there where you're So, OK. I told the doctor. He gave me a paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they took me into that kommando to work. So I was working inside, in a building. It was not anymore the same as I was working there before. And there I worked till the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The electrical power went off as you were speaking, Leon. And you mentioned that you stayed at Auschwitz until-- until what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in the new job until a day before the Russians came in. Then that night before, when we came home from work, they told us we can heat up the soup. And right come back on the appelplatz-- on the platz. And we had to go out right away. And they said, what we want we can take from the camp with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we talked. We figured, it's cold, it's winter, we're going someplace. So I took the quilt, what I covered up with. And everybody took a quilt, or whatever they could, could take. They didn't want to leave it for the Russians, you see. Do you remember the date when you left Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18th of January, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long had you been in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, it was in July 7th-- '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after you got the quilt?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came out from the barracks after supper. And they put us in lines. And marched out from the camp with us with machine guns on both sides. And we walked for two days. Two nights. It was night time. Two nights and two days. We came to the town Gleiwitz. It was a German town. And there was a camp, empty, was evacuated already-- and the people from there. And they let us in there that evening. And everybody ran into, who could get a bed to lay down, who could get the floor, whatever it is. Everybody laid down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next day, I don't think so-- that they gave us some food-- no, they didn't. They had nothing. So they-- they thought, what to do with us? And they take another day, and the Russians were really close, shooting into the town, with machine guns, near our camp. So we know the Russians are here, already-- they took over Auschwitz and came here. So we didn't know what they think to do with us. But that night, at about 12 o'clock, they chased us all out from the barracks. And once they chased us all out, they still went and looked if somebody didn't hide. So they went around all over, for who was caught hiding and shot him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were staying outside the train. Open wagons, like taking coal and things. Open the top. And they pushed us in like sardines. You couldn't even turn around. Standing, all. And I-- good thing, because I was on the edge of the wagon, so I kept my hand holding up here. And then in the center, they started to fall, because it was pushing from one side to the other side. And nighttime was the worst, when you froze. It was winter, it was cold-- you froze. And no food, no nothing. You couldn't-- no toilet, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people in the center-- everybody got tired and fell asleep. So the people in the center, if they got weak a little, the others from the sides fell on top of them. Because everybody was weak.

speaker: interviewee
text: When you stay in the side, you could feel like the-- less air goes out from the people, you see. And they hollered, get up, I am dying, here. I am dying. I can't get any breath. And nobody got the power, the strength, to stand up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every day in the morning, there was about 20, 30 dead, on every one. We got so used to the dead. We put them on lines by the edge and we sit down on them. There was no food, there was nothing. We was traveling with the train-- first we traveled about eight days. And after the eighth day, they told us to throw down all the dead people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took, from every wagon, a couple people, to force them down that they should go bury them. Or not bury them-- to put gasoline and burn them. And then they let the two people back up on the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we were traveling farther with the train and we passed Vienna-- Vienna, you know? When we saw we were passing the city of Vienna-- so, as many as we could, we threw them overboard to the city, so the people should see what's going on. We had frozen hands. We couldn't even move our fingers. But we threw them down as good as we could. So they stopped the train, because the police from the city probably called in to the transport. And they said, if we throw down one more, they're going to shoot the whole train and kill everybody. So we were frightened to do that. And after another couple of days, they stopped on a station-- and at that station was staying a train with military Germans. So those guards probably-- probably, I think so-- went down to them and said we can't take it anymore. It's already been 10, 11 days, people running without water, without food. How can we do that? They probably said to them. So they opened a wagon with bread, and they threw down on every wagon, a whole bread. So we divided it in pieces, for everyone a piece. Where did this bread come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the German army. So-- on the station there. And then we traveled farther. This, we came to Dora-- there's allowed to stop in Dora. They took 14 or 15 days. And I think there was no more survivors than 10%. And the survivors are half-dead. When we went down from the train, they didn't let us in in any barrack. First we had to throw all the dead people down from the train, and bring them on a place where they put up gasoline and burned everything. Then they let us in in the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came in in a barrack, but there was no beds. There was straw all around. But at least it was warm inside. We laid down. And then they brought us bread. I couldn't eat it because everything was frozen inside. I ate snow on the way. So I couldn't-- so my friend from Canada-- what was now in Canada, but he died last year-- so he met there a guy, a Polish guy, a Catholic, what he was in Auschwitz with him. And he was there already about half a year. So he was there a cook in the kitchen. So he came to see who came. Maybe he knows somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he said to him, listen, you work in the kitchen. You have to help me. I have here a cousin, and he can't eat. Everything is frozen by him. You got the bread-- he can't eat. He can't take it down. You have to help. Come with me, he said. He went with him into the kitchen. And gave him a package of butter. He said, push him in the butter, you're going to lubricate everything. And I pushed in the butter. It started to lubricate, and everything started to go, and everything work, and I could eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wisniewski. He's not alive anymore. He died two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-S-N-I-E-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the camp Dora like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp Dora was terrible. They made there the V-1 and V-2, what they sent bombers to England without people. There's a work-- this was in the mountains, the factory. Who works in the mountains was not so bad-- at least it was inside. And in the top of the mountains was our camp. If the Americans supposed to bomb the factories, they had to kill us all first.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked outside. We put rails, so they should be able, from the mountains, out to bring out the bombs. So we had to make the rails outside. It was winter-- it was in February. And my whole fingers were frozen from the transport. And after they got warm, there would become holes in all the fingers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there I had to work there in the cold weather. And they pushed you and beat you. So finally, there was a Polish foreman one-- he came in the morning to work. He said, the Jewish are separate and the other separate. He gave the Jews a heavier work. And I knew that guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told him, listen. Everybody wants to survive. You see it's the end of the war. It's not going to take long. You see the Americans, watch them shoot down on us on the head here. And nobody's shooting them. So they're going to come in any day. What are you doing? So he picked up his whip and he wanted to hit me over the head. So from my nerves, so I caught the whip in my hand. And he wanted to draw out-- he fell. And he hurt himself, he got blood. So came a Gestapo man was standing there, he came, what is here going on? So he said, that Jew said that Germany's going to lose the war right away and they're going to kill me. So he said, if we lose the war, you're not going to be here anymore, he said to me. And he took me away from there. And there was a kommando what's a drop stones-- you know cement, like this long. Like for making the sidewalks, for the edge? Two people took one and go from one place to another, carrying it. No food all day. Only the evening, they got a little soup for all that. So he said to me, you're going to take alone a stone like this. And my nerves, because I was frightened, picked it up-- otherwise, I would never be able to pick it up. I picked it up on my shoulder. And I was going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was going in the back of me. And stepped on my shoes, my wooden shoes that were too big on me. He stepped on my shoes. And he said, if you drop that stone on my feet, I'm going to shoot you. Either way you're going to come only to the crematorium. And a whole day I was going like that. And on the way back, he chased me, I should run. Not working. Run. And whoever was staying on the way, any German, civvy or soldier, any-- they had, a cane or something-- they hit me over the head. And in the evening, I came home. I felt-- my arm, I can't straighten our, because somebody must have hit me here in the bone. I got an inflammation. So I came up. I ate the soup. And I said to the leader of the block, listen. I can't go tomorrow to work. Look on my arm. I can't straighten it out. I understand you, he said. But I can't leave you-- I have no authority to leave you. If you want to be left, you have to go to the hospital, and get from-- a permit from the doctor. And the hospital is closed already. So I had to go to work. The next day, he was waiting for me. The same thing. And I bet if it is a God, if there's some place a God, if someone justice, please, I want to be killed. I don't want to live anymore. It's the end of the war, I know, but I can't hold on anymore. I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came back that day. My friend, that Wisniewski, told me, listen Leon, I told the block leader I should leave you the soup. You don't go home. Go just to the hospital. And my hand-- arm became like this, crooked. So I went up. There was a Polish doctor just closing up the hospital. So he said, what's wrong with you? I told him, look at my arm. Oh, you have an inflammation. I think we're going to have to cut off the arm. But you don't go home-- you stay right in the hospital. I said, listen, I didn't eat nothing all day. He said, you weren't yet in the barracks? I say, no. And somebody told them to leave you the soup? I said, yes. He said, go eat your soup. And you come back. I'm going to wait for you. He was waiting. And in the morning, they operated on my arm. See this? There was a Russian doctor that operated on me. Also a prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- two days later, down the Germans were running away. So they took all the people with them again on a train. The Americans were close. They ran away. And they took whoever could work from the hospital. They took with them. And the rest was left. And they wanted to take me, too. But I didn't want to go. I hid there in the hospital, and I was staying with sick people. And I saw they're going down from the tribune-- they stay with machine guns on us. Everybody goes down, takes the luggage, and runs away. I say, maybe now is a chance to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were staying there two days. And then came back the Germany Wehrmacht. They came back-- well, they ran away from the front. And they saw inside there's kitchens. They had to cook. So they had no place where to cook for the people. the sick people. They came in, and they cooked there-- they had food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us down that we should help him to give us food, too. They were saying there two days. On the third day, in the morning, we came down and saw the officers left all their uniforms and dressed as civilians and ran away. And everybody ran away. It took another day-- the next day, the Americans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when the Americans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know how I felt. I felt I'm liberated, but in the minute, I didn't know what to say. They came in a tank, in the camp. We couldn't speak to them. I didn't speak any English. I couldn't speak a word. There was one already from another prison camp on the tank. And he knew English. So he was the interpreter. And they told us, on the tank, just a fresh patrol comes in here. And tomorrow comes in the whole occupation army. And they're going to take, two or three days, take care of the camp-- the Americans. So we should have patience. But the next day, in the morning, I walked out right away from the camp. I walked away with another guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date that you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 11th, 1945. That is my date what I always make a party. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think sustained you during your years in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I think?

speaker: interviewer
text: What sustained you? What kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: My strength? What keeps me? I had a very, very much will to live. I always hoped it would not take forever. Maybe somebody can survive. But sometimes there came such a moment, that we're so depressed, I couldn't take it anymore. I wanted to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left. We went into the city. On the whole road, we're staying with American tanks-- both sides. It was not yet the end of the war. And I couldn't speak to the Americans, you see. They came to saw me, here, the arms, they said, who did you that?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- as much I could explain, I explained to them. I said, they beat me up. So the soldiers say, are you hungry? They say, they don't have anything to eat because the kitchen is going to come in an hour. So they have wine. [LAUGHS] So we drink a bottle of wine. And I AND my friend, with a friend I went, we drank up the wine. We got drunk. [LAUGHS] You know, after such years, and you felt, wine, you couldn't get the way of it. So-- we got drunk, both of us. So we went to the town, to the city. We came to the city. So we were drunk. And there was staying a lady, an older lady. And she spoke Polish. So I say, how are you speaking Polish here? How? She said, she came here years ago and she got married here, because it was bad in Poland, so she came here. But I speak Polish. So she invited us up to the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Americans were in the yard. There was staying four Americans with barrels of wine. And they're staying, pumping wine with a pump. Oh, the American boys. And we went up upstairs. So I came up. I see they're not Polish! They're German soldiers-- the whole family. [LAUGHS] I say-- so, they're cooking dinner. I say, listen, give us something to eat. So she said, she had to cook for her husband and son. The Americans took them away to work someplace. I said, he did eat all the time. We didn't eat for six years, come on! So she said, get the hell away from me here. We went to the kitchen, we took away what she had on the-- and put it on the table. Because we're hungry. And we ate. Then she didn't say anything more. She was frightened of us. I said, don't be frightened. We ate up, and then we went down. We went down. So the Americans were staying pumping wine. So my friend goes down with a cup. He's like, pump it in. So he said, no, we come from a concentration camp, we've got the uniform still. So he would start the pump but another guy was smarter. He came back and said, no, I won't give you any wine. You're going to get sick. So we walked out from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: As we were walking out, we faced a tank. And he said, are you Jewish? In Yiddish. Said [YIDDISH]. [LAUGHS] You know? Were four boys from New York, Jewish all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sat down on the tank with them, and they started to find out what's happening during the war. And I told him everything. And they-- and I-- and we spoke with them for about an hour. And then I told them, listen, somebody told me there's a possibility that the Americans can get thrown out from here, because the war is still going on. I don't want to go back to the camp. I'm going to stay here with you. If you're moving, I go with you. I don't want to go back here anymore. He said, no, don't worry. We are here to stay. We will never move out from here. And they took us in that we should go back to sleep. We went back to the camp to sleep. And the next I know, we went with an American jeep to Buchenwald. They said, from there, going to transport to Switzerland. Maybe we'd be able to go. They're taking young people to Switzerland. When I came there, I found a list of my two cousins are in-- in that other camp-- what the name is. And we went there by train. And on the way, we stopped in another camp and I found my friend from that camp. [LAUGHS] He we went away with the Germans. He was there. He thought I'm not alive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I met my two cousins there. One was in the hospital with typhus. And the other one helped him out. And then my friend with me, we married the two girls later. [LAUGHS] And she is still with him, and I'm here alone. Who is it that you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: How?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you married one of the girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. that's Mary. And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sontag, too. And the other one is Frania-- she is in Canada. She comes here for the winter. She lives there in Aventura-- they have an apartment. Flamingo. You know where Flamingo is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after the war, when we came to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a manager in a big corporation of clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, my wife, she found out all her family, what they ran away to Russia when the war started, they ran away from the front. They came into Russia with the kids and everybody for six years. They came back home, and see nobody is home. So they went to America. And they're in America, so she wanted to go, too, to America. Because of her, we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to your hometown after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Twice. I was in Gdynia, I was in Dabie after the war. Once I was in 1987, and I the other time I was I think in '91.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to America--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people ask you about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, yes. Some asked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, it was tough on me. I set up with a guy that had a shop for sewing clothing. And he took me in and did me a favor. Took me in. I don't complain on him. But he gave me the chance to make at least a few dollars. And then I stopped. I'd been with him about three, four months. I went right to school to learn how the measurements go different from Europe and here. And I learned three months [INAUDIBLE]. I got right to a position where I make money, a pattern maker in ladies' clothing. I'd been with [INAUDIBLE] for a few years. Then I was working in different places. And then I went to business myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have tuxedo rentals for weddings.
